{{Short description|Mood disorder characterized by emotional, cognitive, and physical symptoms}}
{{Infobox medical condition
| name          = Premenstrual dysphoric disorder
| synonyms      = Late luteal phase dysphoric disorder
| image         = 
| alt           = 
| caption       = 
| pronounce     = 
| field         = [[Psychiatry]]
| geneReviewsID = 
| symptoms      = [[Dysphoria]], severe mood swings, [[depression (mood)|depression]], irritability, agitation, [[Malaise|uneasiness]], change in appetite, [[fatigue|severe fatigue]], [[anxiety]], anger insomnia/hypersomnia, breast tenderness, decreased interest in usual social activities, reduced interest in sexual activity, difficulty in concentration
| complications = 
| onset         = Can occur anytime during reproductive years
| duration      = 6 days – 3 weeks of cycle
| types         = 
| causes        = Likely neuro-sensitivity to reproductive hormones
| risks         = Family history, history of violence/trauma, smoking, presence of other mental health disorders
| diagnosis     = Based on symptoms & criteria
| differential  = [[Premenstrual syndrome]], [[Major depressive disorder|depression]], [[anxiety disorder]]
| prevention    = 
| treatment     = Medication, [[Psychotherapy|counselling]], lifestyle change,  surgery
| medication    = [[SSRI]]s, [[drospirenone]]-containing [[oral contraceptive]]s, [[GnRH analog]]s, [[cognitive behavioral therapy]] (CBT)
| frequency     = Up to about 8% of menstruating women
| deaths        = 
}}
<!-- Definition and symptoms -->

'''Premenstrual dysphoric disorder''' ('''PMDD''') is a [[mood disorder]] characterized by emotional, cognitive, and physical symptoms. PMDD can cause significant distress and impairment during the [[luteal phase]] of the [[menstrual cycle]] (between [[ovulation]] and menstruation). Symptoms typically improve within a few days after the onset of menses, and are minimal or absent in the week after menses.<ref name="DSM5">{{Cite book|title=Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders|publisher=American Psychiatric Association|year=2013|location=Arlington, VA|pages=625.4|edition=5th}} Code: 625.4 (N94.3)</ref> PMDD has a profound impact on an individual's quality of life and dramatically increases the risk of [[suicidal ideation]] and even [[suicide attempt]]s.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Prasad|first1=Divya|last2=Wollenhaupt-Aguiar|first2=Bianca|last3=Kidd|first3=Katrina N.|last4=de Azevedo Cardoso|first4=Taiane|last5=Frey|first5=Benicio N.|date=December 2021|title=Suicidal Risk in Women with Premenstrual Syndrome and Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis|journal=Journal of Women's Health |volume=30|issue=12|pages=1693–1707|doi=10.1089/jwh.2021.0185|issn=1931-843X|pmc=8721500|pmid=34415776}}</ref><ref name="Hodgetts">{{cite journal |last1=Hodgetts |first1=Sophie |last2=Kinghorn |first2=Aaron |title=Examining the impact of premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) on life and relationship quality: An online cross-sectional survey study |journal=PLOS ONE |date=2025-04-23 |volume=20 |issue=4 |page=13-14 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0322314 |doi-access=free |pmid=40267093 |pmc=12017513 |bibcode=2025PLoSO..2022314H }}</ref> Many women experience discomfort or mild mood changes before menstruation, but 5–8% experience severe [[premenstrual syndrome]] (PMS), causing significant distress or functional impairment.<ref name=yonkers/> Within this group, some will meet the criteria for PMDD.<ref>{{cite web |title=Premenstrual dysphoric disorder: Different from PMS? |url=https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/premenstrual-syndrome/expert-answers/pmdd/faq-20058315?p=1 |website=Mayo Clinic |date=2024}}</ref> <!-- Cause and diagnosis -->

PMDD's exact cause is unknown. Ovarian hormone levels during the menstrual cycle do not differ between those with PMDD and the general population.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hofmeister |first1=Sabrina |last2=Bodden |first2=Seth |date=2016-08-01 |title=Premenstrual Syndrome and Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder |journal=American Family Physician |volume=94 |issue=3 |pages=236–240 |issn=1532-0650 |pmid=27479626}}</ref> But because symptoms are present only during ovulatory cycles and resolve after menstruation, it is believed to be caused by fluctuations in gonadal [[sex hormone]]s or variations in sensitivity to sex hormones.<ref name=":9" />

In 2017, [[National Institutes of Health]] researchers discovered that women with PMDD have genetic changes that make their emotional regulatory pathways more sensitive to [[estrogen]] and [[progesterone]], as well as their chemical derivatives. The researchers believe this increased sensitivity may cause PMDD symptoms.<ref name=":64">{{cite journal | vauthors = Dubey N, Hoffman JF, Schuebel K, Yuan Q, Martinez PE, Nieman LK, Rubinow DR, Schmidt PJ, Goldman D | display-authors = 6 | title = The ESC/E(Z) complex, an effector of response to ovarian steroids, manifests an intrinsic difference in cells from women with premenstrual dysphoric disorder | journal = Molecular Psychiatry | volume = 22 | issue = 8 | pages = 1172–1184 | date = August 2017 | pmid = 28044059 | pmc = 5495630 | doi = 10.1038/mp.2016.229 }}</ref>

Studies have found that those with PMDD are more at risk of developing [[postpartum depression]] after pregnancy.<ref name=":7">{{cite journal |vauthors=Yang Q, Bränn E, Bertone-Johnson ER, Sjölander A, Fang F, Oberg AS, Valdimarsdóttir UA, Lu D |title=The bidirectional association between premenstrual disorders and perinatal depression: A nationwide register-based study from Sweden |journal=PLOS Med |volume=21 |issue=3 |article-number=e1004363 |date=March 2024 |pmid=38547436 |pmc=10978009 |doi=10.1371/journal.pmed.1004363 |doi-access=free }}</ref> PMDD was added to the list of depressive disorders in the ''[[Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders]]'' in 2013.<ref name="DSM5" /> It has 11 main symptoms, of which five must be present for a PMDD diagnosis.<ref name=":9">{{cite journal | vauthors = Pearlstein T | title = Treatment of Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder: Therapeutic Challenges | journal = Expert Review of Clinical Pharmacology | volume = 9 | issue = 4 | pages = 493–496 | date = April 2016 | pmid = 26766596 | doi = 10.1586/17512433.2016.1142371 | s2cid = 12172042 | doi-access = free }}</ref> Roughly 20% of females have some PMDD symptoms, but either have fewer than five or do not have functional impairment.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Steiner M, Macdougall M, Brown E | title = The premenstrual symptoms screening tool (PSST) for clinicians | journal = Archives of Women's Mental Health | volume = 6 | issue = 3 | pages = 203–9 | date = August 2003 | pmid = 12920618 | doi = 10.1007/s00737-003-0018-4 | s2cid = 24822881 }}</ref>

<!-- Treatment -->
The first-line treatment for PMDD is with [[selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors]] (SSRIs), which can be administered continuously throughout the menstrual cycle or intermittently, with treatment only during the symptomatic phase (approximately 14 days per cycle).<ref name="Rapkin">{{cite journal|vauthors=Rapkin AJ, Lewis EI|date=November 2013|title=Treatment of premenstrual dysphoric disorder|journal=Women's Health|volume=9|issue=6|pages=537–56|doi=10.2217/whe.13.62|pmid=24161307|doi-access=free|s2cid=45517684}}</ref> Hormonal therapy with [[oral contraceptive]]s that contain [[drospirenone]] have also demonstrated efficiency in reducing PMDD symptoms.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lopez|first1=Laureen M.|last2=Kaptein|first2=Ad A.|last3=Helmerhorst|first3=Frans M.|editor1-first=Laureen M|editor1-last=Lopez|date=2009-04-15|title=Oral contraceptives containing drospirenone for premenstrual syndrome|journal=The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews|issue=2|article-number=CD006586|doi=10.1002/14651858.CD006586.pub3|issn=1469-493X|pmid=19370644}}</ref> [[Cognitive behavioral therapy]], whether in combination with SSRIs or alone, has shown to be effective in reducing impairment.<ref name="ReferenceA">{{cite journal|vauthors=Kleinstäuber M, Witthöft M, Hiller W|date=September 2012|title=Cognitive-behavioral and pharmacological interventions for premenstrual syndrome or premenstrual dysphoric disorder: a meta-analysis|journal=Journal of Clinical Psychology in Medical Settings|volume=19|issue=3|pages=308–19|doi=10.1007/s10880-012-9299-y|pmid=22426857|s2cid=28720541}}</ref> Dietary modifications and exercise may also be helpful, but studies investigating these treatments have not demonstrated efficacy in reducing PMDD symptoms.<ref name="Rapkin"/>

==Signs and symptoms==
Clinicians consider mood symptoms, physical symptoms, and impact on the patient's life in making the diagnosis of PMDD. Mood symptoms include [[dysphoria]], emotional lability (rapidly changing emotions, sensitivity to rejection, etc.), irritability and anger that may lead to conflict, anxiety, feeling on edge, hopelessness, difficulty concentrating, appetite changes, sleeping more or less than usual, or feeling out of control. The physical symptoms are similar to the symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as breast tenderness or swelling, joint pain, muscle pain, weight gain, and [[bloating]].<ref name="DSM5" />

Because of the broad variety in clinical presentation, the onset of symptoms only during or around the luteal phase is key for diagnosing someone with PMDD rather than any other mood disorders.<ref name=":8">{{cite journal|vauthors=Reid RL, Soares CN|date=February 2018|title=Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder: Contemporary Diagnosis and Management|journal=Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Canada|volume=40|issue=2|pages=215–223|doi=10.1016/j.jogc.2017.05.018|pmid=29132964}}</ref> PMDD follows a predictable, cyclic pattern. Symptoms begin in the late [[luteal phase]] of the [[menstrual cycle]] (after ovulation) and end or are markedly reduced shortly after menstruation begins.<ref name=":53">{{cite journal | vauthors = Steiner M, Pearlstein T, Cohen LS, Endicott J, Kornstein SG, Roberts C, Roberts DL, Yonkers K | display-authors = 6 | title = Expert guidelines for the treatment of severe PMS, PMDD, and comorbidities: the role of SSRIs | journal = Journal of Women's Health | volume = 15 | issue = 1 | pages = 57–69 | date = 2006 | pmid = 16417420 | doi = 10.1089/jwh.2006.15.57 }}</ref> On average, the symptoms last six days but can start up to two weeks before menses, meaning symptoms can be felt for up to three weeks out of a cycle. Severe symptoms can begin and worsen until the onset of menstruation, with many not feeling relief until a few days after menstruation ends. The most intense symptoms occur in the week and days leading up to the first day of menstrual blood flow.<ref name=yonkers>{{cite journal|vauthors=Yonkers KA, O'Brien PM, Eriksson E|date=April 2008|title=Premenstrual syndrome|journal=Lancet|volume=371|issue=9619|pages=1200–10|doi=10.1016/S0140-6736(08)60527-9|pmc=3118460|pmid=18395582}}</ref> The symptoms usually cease shortly after the start of the menstrual period or a few days after it ends.<ref name="Rapkin" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Biggs WS, Demuth RH | title = Premenstrual syndrome and premenstrual dysphoric disorder | journal = American Family Physician | volume = 84 | issue = 8 | pages = 918–24 | date = October 2011 | pmid = 22010771 }}</ref> Various symptom and severity tracking questionnaires exist to document presence and severity of symptoms throughout consecutive menstrual cycles.<ref name="eisenlohr" /><ref name=":8" />

The International Society for the Study of Premenstrual Disorders (ISPMD) defines two categories of premenstrual disorders: core PMD and variant PMD.

Core PMD has six characteristics, mainly focusing on the cyclical nature of PMDD and its typical onset pre-menses tracked for more than two menstrual cycles. The four classified variant PMDs involve more unexpected variables that cause the onset of premenstrual distress, such as PMD with absent menstruation or premenstrual exacerbation, wherein the symptoms of another preexisting psychological disorder may be heightened as a result of PMDD onset.<ref name=":8" />

== Epidemiology ==
About 5-8% of women who are of reproductive age experience severe [[premenstrual syndrome]]; most of them also meet the criteria for PMDD.<ref name=yonkers />

==Pathophysiology ==
PMDD mood symptoms are only present in menstruating women. Thus, symptoms do not occur during pregnancy, after menopause, or in women who have anovulatory cycles. Other mood disorders typically persist across all reproductive life events and are independent of a woman's menstrual cycle.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Douma SL, Husband C, O'Donnell ME, Barwin BN, Woodend AK | title = Estrogen-related mood disorders: reproductive life cycle factors | journal = Advances in Nursing Science | volume = 28 | issue = 4 | pages = 364–75 | date = October 2005 | pmid = 16292022 | doi = 10.1097/00012272-200510000-00008 | s2cid = 9172877 }}</ref>

The current consensus on the cause of PMDD is a combination of heightened sensitivity to fluctuating levels of certain hormones (i.e., the reproductive hormones), environmental stress, and genetic predisposition.<ref name=":8" /> The sex steroids—[[estrogen]] and [[progesterone]]—are neuroactive; they have been noted in rat models to be involved in serotonin pathways.<ref name=":8" /> [[Serotonin]] is involved in mood regulation alongside estrogen, whose receptors are found in the [[prefrontal cortex]] and [[hippocampus]]—the regions most known for their involvement in regulating one's mood and cognition overall.<ref name=yonkers /><ref name=":8" />

While the timing of symptoms suggests hormonal fluctuations as the cause of PMDD, a demonstrable hormonal imbalance in women with PMDD has not been identified. Levels of reproductive hormones and their metabolites in women with and without PMDD are indistinguishable.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Rubinow DR, Schmidt PJ | title = Gonadal steroid regulation of mood: the lessons of premenstrual syndrome | journal = Frontiers in Neuroendocrinology | volume = 27 | issue = 2 | pages = 210–6 | date = July 2006 | pmid = 16650465 | doi = 10.1016/j.yfrne.2006.02.003 | s2cid = 8268435 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Nguyen TV, Reuter JM, Gaikwad NW, Rotroff DM, Kucera HR, Motsinger-Reif A, Smith CP, Nieman LK, Rubinow DR, Kaddurah-Daouk R, Schmidt PJ | display-authors = 6 | title = The steroid metabolome in women with premenstrual dysphoric disorder during GnRH agonist-induced ovarian suppression: effects of estradiol and progesterone addback | journal = Translational Psychiatry | volume = 7 | issue = 8 | pages = e1193 | date = August 2017 | pmid = 28786978 | pmc = 5611719 | doi = 10.1038/tp.2017.146 }}</ref><ref name=":5">{{cite journal | vauthors = Hantsoo L, Epperson CN | title = Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder: Epidemiology and Treatment | journal = Current Psychiatry Reports | volume = 17 | issue = 11 | article-number = 87 | date = November 2015 | pmid = 26377947 | pmc = 4890701 | doi = 10.1007/s11920-015-0628-3 }}</ref> It is instead hypothesized that women with PMDD are more sensitive to normal levels of hormone fluctuations, predominantly estrogen and progesterone, which produces biochemical events in the nervous system that cause the premenstrual symptoms.<ref name=":5" /> These symptoms are more predominant in women with a predisposition to the disorder.<ref name=":53" />

It is apparent that premenstrual disorders are biologically driven and are not simply psychological or cultural phenomena. PMDD has been reported by menstruating women worldwide, indicating a biological basis that is not geographically selective.<ref name=yonkers /> Most psychologists infer that this disorder is caused by both a reaction to hormone flux and also genetic components. There is evidence of heritability of (retrospectively reported) premenstrual symptoms from several twin and family studies done in the 1990s, with the heritability of PMDD proving to be about 56%.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kendler KS, Karkowski LM, Corey LA, Neale MC | title = Longitudinal population-based twin study of retrospectively reported premenstrual symptoms and lifetime major depression | journal = The American Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 155 | issue = 9 | pages = 1234–40 | date = September 1998 | pmid = 9734548 | doi = 10.1176/ajp.155.9.1234 | s2cid = 2048753 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Condon JT | title = The premenstrual syndrome: a twin study | journal = The British Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 162 | issue = 4 | pages = 481–6 | date = April 1993 | pmid = 8481739 | doi = 10.1192/bjp.162.4.481 | publisher = Cambridge University Press | s2cid = 27525780 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Wilson CA, Turner CW, Keye WR | title = Firstborn adolescent daughters and mothers with and without premenstrual syndrome: a comparison | journal = The Journal of Adolescent Health | volume = 12 | issue = 2 | pages = 130–7 | date = March 1991 | pmid = 2015237 | doi = 10.1016/0197-0070(91)90455-U }}</ref>

=== Genetic factors ===
Whether or not this disorder has a specific genetic basis is still being discussed in the academic community. The possible genetic factors contributing to PMDD also have yet to be thoroughly researched. However, multiple genetic factors that contribute to the moodiness, depression, irritability, increased appetite, trouble sleeping, [[acne]], fluid retention, headaches, nausea, and other symptoms associated with this disorder have recently been identified.{{Citation needed|reason=Not sure if claims above are reflected in citations in subsequent paragraphs or not. Also, it's unclear when these genetic factors were identified|date=February 2023}}

Many studies have noted that a polymorphism of the brain-derived [[Brain-derived neurotrophic factor|neurotrophic factor gene]] (BDNF), a gene that helps support neurons in their function and survival in the brain by creating a protein that helps in the growth, maturation, and maintenance of these cells, may play a role in causing PMDD symptoms. This is because the result of this polymorphism mimics the hallmarks of PMDD: volatile moods, depression, and irritability centered around the menstrual cycle. This gene has been studied extensively in its association with depression and, promisingly for PMDD research, mice homozygous for the BDNF polymorphism exhibited anxiety-like traits that fluctuated and changed around the mice's estrus, analogous to the human's menstruation, therefore mimicking some of the symptoms of PMDD.<ref name=":6">{{cite journal | vauthors = McEvoy K, Osborne LM, Nanavati J, Payne JL | title = Reproductive Affective Disorders: a Review of the Genetic Evidence for Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder and Postpartum Depression | journal = Current Psychiatry Reports | volume = 19 | issue = 12 | article-number = 94 | date = October 2017 | pmid = 29082433 | doi = 10.1007/s11920-017-0852-0 | s2cid = 21658798 }}</ref>

=== Risk factors ===
Environmental stressors have also been found to increase the risk for PMDD symptoms prospectively.<ref>{{cite journal|vauthors=Namavar Jahromi B, Pakmehr S, Hagh-Shenas H|date=March 2011|title=Work stress, premenstrual syndrome and dysphoric disorder: are there any associations?|journal=Iranian Red Crescent Medical Journal|volume=13|issue=3|pages=199–202|pmc=3371938|pmid=22737463}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|vauthors=Gollenberg AL, Hediger ML, Mumford SL, Whitcomb BW, Hovey KM, Wactawski-Wende J, Schisterman EF|date=May 2010|title=Perceived stress and severity of perimenstrual symptoms: the BioCycle Study|journal=Journal of Women's Health|volume=19|issue=5|pages=959–67|doi=10.1089/jwh.2009.1717|pmc=2875955|pmid=20384452}}</ref> Environmental components such as stress, hormonal fluctuation, and [[epigenetics]] play a key role in the pathology and onset of the disorder.<ref name=":64" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Beddig |first1=Theresa |last2=Reinhard |first2=Iris |last3=Kuehner |first3=Christine |title=Stress, mood, and cortisol during daily life in women with Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder (PMDD) |journal=Psychoneuroendocrinology |date=2019-11-01 |volume=109 |article-number=104372 |doi=10.1016/j.psyneuen.2019.104372 |issn=0306-4530}}</ref> Some studies have noted evidence of interpersonal trauma (domestic violence, physical or emotional trauma, or substance use) or seasonal changes (making PMDD potentially comorbid with [[seasonal affective disorder]]) having an impact on PMDD risk.<ref name="Rapkin" /><ref name=":72">{{cite journal|vauthors=Epperson CN, Steiner M, Hartlage SA, Eriksson E, Schmidt PJ, Jones I, Yonkers KA|date=May 2012|title=Premenstrual dysphoric disorder: evidence for a new category for DSM-5|journal=The American Journal of Psychiatry|volume=169|issue=5|pages=465–75|doi=10.1176/appi.ajp.2012.11081302|pmc=3462360|pmid=22764360}}</ref> But the most common pre-existing disorder found in those diagnosed with PMDD is major depression, wherein they either had it or were misdiagnosed when they should have only been diagnosed with PMDD.<ref name=":72" /> Finally, an easily modifiable risk factor for PMDD is cigarette smoking. One meta-analysis found dramatically increased risk of developing PMDD in menstruating women who smoke.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Choi|first1=So Hee|last2=Hamidovic|first2=Ajna|date=2020|title=Association Between Smoking and Premenstrual Syndrome: A Meta-Analysis|journal=Frontiers in Psychiatry|volume=11|article-number=575526|doi=10.3389/fpsyt.2020.575526|issn=1664-0640|pmc=7725748|pmid=33324253|doi-access=free}}</ref>

=== Relationship to pregnancy and menopause ===
Women with PMDD usually see their symptoms disappear while they are [[Pregnancy|pregnant]]. Premenstrual dysphoric disorder is primarily a mood disorder that is associated with onset of menstruation; pregnancy, menopause, and hysterectomies all cause menstruation to cease, thereby stopping the proposed sex steroid-/serotonin-caused symptoms from occurring.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":16">{{cite journal | vauthors = Kepple AL, Lee EE, Haq N, Rubinow DR, Schmidt PJ | title = History of postpartum depression in a clinic-based sample of women with premenstrual dysphoric disorder | journal = The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry | volume = 77 | issue = 4 | pages = e415-20 | date = April 2016 | pmid = 27035701 | pmc = 6328311 | doi = 10.4088/JCP.15m09779 }}</ref> Although one might expect a higher rate of [[postpartum depression]] among those with PMDD, a large study of women with prospectively confirmed PMDD did not find a higher prevalence of postpartum depression than in controls.<ref name=":7" /><ref name=":16" /> If a woman had experienced PPD beforehand, there was found to be a less-than 12% chance of PMDD pathology emerging—hardly any differentiation from the regular population of those who have never experienced postpartum depression.<ref name=":16" /> However, PMDD symptoms can get worse following pregnancy or other associated events such as birth and miscarriage.<ref name=":222">{{Cite web|date=14 January 2019|title=What is PMDD?|url=https://iapmd.org/about-pmdd|access-date=29 April 2019|website=IAPMD|vauthors=Liisa H}}</ref>

===Mental health comorbidities===
The lifetime incidence of other psychiatric disorders is high among women with PMDD. An older review article (2002) utilizing the previous edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders ([[DSM-IV]]) which used studies from 1966 to 2002 on PMS and mental health disorders and selected for patients who retrospectively met the diagnostic criteria for PMDD found that [[major depressive disorder]], seasonal affective disorder, and generalized anxiety disorder often co-occur in PMDD.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kim|first1=D. R.|last2=Gyulai|first2=L.|last3=Freeman|first3=E. W.|last4=Morrison|first4=M. F.|last5=Baldassano|first5=C.|last6=Dubé|first6=B.|date=February 2004|title=Premenstrual dysphoric disorder and psychiatric co-morbidity|journal=Archives of Women's Mental Health|volume=7|issue=1|pages=37–47|doi=10.1007/s00737-003-0027-3|issn=1434-1816|pmid=14963731|s2cid=2977103}}</ref> Another systematic review study suggests that patients with bipolar disorder, type I or II, have a higher incidence of PMDD.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Cirillo|first1=Patricia Carvalho|last2=Passos|first2=Roberta Benitez Freitas|last3=Bevilaqua|first3=Mario Cesar do Nascimento|last4=López|first4=Jose Ramón Rodriguez Arras|last5=Nardi|first5=Antônio Egidio|date=December 2012|title=Bipolar disorder and Premenstrual Syndrome or Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder comorbidity: a systematic review|journal=Revista Brasileira de Psiquiatria |volume=34|issue=4|pages=467–479|doi=10.1016/j.rbp.2012.04.010|issn=1809-452X|pmid=23429819|doi-access=free}}</ref> While the diagnosis of PMDD requires a mental health provider to determine that the symptoms a woman is facing are not due to an underlying mental or physical health condition, it is important to note that other conditions often co-occur and impact the quality of life and treatment plan for people with PMDD. A 2025 study of the Reddit peer support community r/PMDD found that users showed a decrease in associations with depression and anxiety after engaging in the forum, possibly reflecting a reconfiguration of symptom interpretation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Evkoski |first1=Bojan |last2=Letina |first2=Srebrenka |last3=Kralj Novak |first3=Petra |last4=Riddell |first4=Julie |date=2025 |title=Premenstrual dysphoric disorder in online peer support communities: a Reddit case study |journal=Scientific Reports |volume=15 |pages=34300 |doi=10.1038/s41598-025-34300-3 |doi-broken-date=9 December 2025 |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-34300-3}}</ref>

=== Suicidality ===
Previous links to suicidality and PMS have been made, but women with PMDD are more likely still to consider and attempt suicide even when controlling for mental health comorbidities.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Yan|first1=Haohao|last2=Ding|first2=Yudan|last3=Guo|first3=Wenbin|date=2021-12-01|title=Suicidality in patients with premenstrual dysphoric disorder-A systematic review and meta-analysis|journal=Journal of Affective Disorders|volume=295|pages=339–346|doi=10.1016/j.jad.2021.08.082|issn=1573-2517|pmid=34488087}}</ref> Despite the increase in suicidal ideation and attempts in this population, the data currently suggests that suicidal ideation or action is not more likely to occur during the late luteal phase when PMDD symptoms would occur.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Osborn|first1=E.|last2=Brooks|first2=J.|last3=O'Brien|first3=P. M. S.|last4=Wittkowski|first4=A.|date=April 2021|title=Suicidality in women with Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder: a systematic literature review|journal=Archives of Women's Mental Health|volume=24|issue=2|pages=173–184|doi=10.1007/s00737-020-01054-8|issn=1435-1102|pmc=7979645|pmid=32936329}}</ref> It is difficult to study whether treatment reduces suicidality because of the multifaceted reasons provided for suicidal ideation. However, treatment has been well documented to reduce physical and emotional symptoms of PMDD.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Shah|first1=Nirav R.|last2=Jones|first2=J. B.|last3=Aperi|first3=Jaclyn|last4=Shemtov|first4=Rachel|last5=Karne|first5=Anita|last6=Borenstein|first6=Jeff|date=May 2008|title=Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors for premenstrual syndrome and premenstrual dysphoric disorder: a meta-analysis|journal=Obstetrics and Gynecology|volume=111|issue=5|pages=1175–1182|doi=10.1097/AOG.0b013e31816fd73b|issn=0029-7844|pmc=2670364|pmid=18448752}}</ref><ref name="Rapkin" />

==Diagnosis==
Several expert medical guides provide diagnostic criteria for PMDD. Diagnosis can be supported by having women who are seeking treatment for PMDD use a daily charting method to record their symptoms.<ref name=":8" /> Daily charting helps to distinguish when mood disturbances are experienced and allows PMDD to be more easily distinguished from other mood disorders. With PMDD, mood symptoms are present only during the luteal phase, or the last two weeks, of the menstrual cycle.<ref name=":53" /> While PMDD mood symptoms are cyclical, other mood disorders are variable or constant over time. Although there is a lack of consensus on the most efficient instrument by which to confirm a PMDD diagnosis, several validated scales for recording premenstrual symptoms include the Calendar of Premenstrual Experiences (COPE), Daily Record of Severity of Problems (DRSP), and Prospective Record of the Severity of Menstruation (PRISM).<ref name="eisenlohr" /><ref name=":19" /> In the context of research, standardized numerical cutoffs are often applied to verify the diagnosis.<ref name="eisenlohr" /> The difficulty of diagnosing PMDD is one reason that it can be challenging for lawyers to cite the disorder as a defense of crime in the very rare cases where PMDD is allegedly associated with criminal violence.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20191213-pmdd-a-little-understood-and-often-misdiagnosed-condition|title=The overlooked condition that can trigger extreme behaviour| vauthors = Ro C |website=www.bbc.com|date=16 December 2019 |language=en|access-date=2020-01-04}}</ref>

===DSM-5===
The [[DSM-5]] established seven criteria (A through G) for the diagnosis of PMDD, which are paraphrased below.<ref name="DSM5" /> There is overlap between the criteria for PMDD in the DSM-5 and the criteria found in the Daily Record of Severity of Problems (DRSP).<ref name="eisenlohr">{{cite journal | vauthors = Eisenlohr-Moul TA, Girdler SS, Schmalenberger KM, Dawson DN, Surana P, Johnson JL, Rubinow DR | title = Toward the Reliable Diagnosis of DSM-5 Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder: The Carolina Premenstrual Assessment Scoring System (C-PASS) | journal = The American Journal of Psychiatry | volume = 174 | issue = 1 | pages = 51–59 | date = January 2017 | pmid = 27523500 | pmc = 5205545 | doi = 10.1176/appi.ajp.2016.15121510 }}</ref><ref name=":19">{{cite journal | vauthors = Endicott J, Nee J, Harrison W | title = Daily Record of Severity of Problems (DRSP): reliability and validity | journal = Archives of Women's Mental Health | volume = 9 | issue = 1 | pages = 41–9 | date = January 2006 | pmid = 16172836 | doi = 10.1007/s00737-005-0103-y | s2cid = 25479566 }}</ref>

According to the DSM-5, a diagnosis of PMDD requires the presence of at least five of these symptoms, with one of the symptoms being numbers 1–4. These symptoms should occur during the week before menses and remit after initiation of menses. To meet the criteria for the diagnosis, the symptoms should be charted prospectively for two consecutive ovulation cycles to confirm the temporal and cyclical nature of the symptoms. The symptoms should also be severe enough to affect normal work, school, social activities, and/or relationships with others.

The symptoms of Criteria A-C must have been met for most menstrual cycles that occurred in the preceding year and must have caused significant impairment in family, work, school, or social functioning. (''Criterion D'').

'''<u>Timing</u>'''

'''''Criterion A''''': During most menstrual cycles throughout the past year, at least five of the symptoms outlined in Criterion B and Criterion C must be present in the final week before the onset of menses, must start to improve within a few days after the onset of menses, and become minimal or absent in the week post-menses.

'''<u>Symptoms</u>'''

'''''Criterion B:''''' One (or more) of the following symptoms must be present:

# Marked [[Pseudobulbar affect|affective lability]] (e.g., mood swings, feeling suddenly sad or tearful, or increased sensitivity to rejection)
# Marked irritability or anger or increased interpersonal conflicts
# Marked depressed mood, feelings of hopelessness, or self-deprecating thoughts
# Marked anxiety, tension, and/or feelings of being keyed up or on edge

'''''Criterion C:''''' One (or more) of the following symptoms must be present additionally, to reach a total of 5 symptoms when combined with present symptoms from ''Criterion B'' above:<ref name="DSM5" />

# Decreased interest in usual activities (e.g., work, school, friends, hobbies).
# Subjective difficulty in concentration.
# Lethargy, easy fatigability, or marked lack of energy.
# Marked change in appetite; overeating; or specific food cravings.
# Hypersomnia or insomnia. 
# A sense of being overwhelmed or out of control.
# Physical symptoms such as breast tenderness or swelling, joint or muscle pain, a sensation of "bloating", or weight gain.

'''<u>Severity</u>'''

'''''Criterion D:''''' The symptoms observed in Criteria A-C are associated with clinically significant distress or interference with work, school, usual social activities, or relationships with others (e.g., avoidance of social activities; decreased productivity and efficiency at work, school, or home).

<u>'''Consideration of Other Psychiatric Disorders'''</u>

'''''Criterion E'':''' The disturbance is not merely an exacerbation of the symptoms of another disorder, such as [[major depressive disorder]], [[panic disorder]], persistent depressive disorder ([[dysthymia]]), or a [[personality disorder]]—although it may co-occur with any of these disorders.<ref name="DSM5" />

'''<u>Confirmation of the Disorder</u>'''

'''''Criterion F'':''' Criterion A should be confirmed by prospective daily ratings during at least two symptomatic cycles. The diagnosis may be made provisionally before this confirmation.

'''''Criterion G'':''' The symptoms are not attributable to the physiological effects of a substance (e.g., [[substance use disorder]], a medication, other treatments) or another medical condition (e.g., [[hyperthyroidism]]).

Multiple scholars have critiqued the criterion of significant distress as being too vague. The [[Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders|DSM-IV]] does not explicitly define this term, and scholars have viewed this criterion as potentially detrimental for those who have symptoms of depression, anxiety, or other mood disorders because they do not meet the clinical significance requirement.<ref>{{cite journal|vauthors=Spitzer RL, Wakefield JC|date=December 1999|title=DSM-IV diagnostic criterion for clinical significance: does it help solve the false positives problem?|journal=The American Journal of Psychiatry|volume=156|issue=12|pages=1856–64|doi=10.1176/ajp.156.12.1856|pmid=10588397|s2cid=25642814 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|display-authors=6|vauthors=Grenier S, Préville M, Boyer R, O'Connor K, Béland SG, Potvin O, Hudon C, Brassard J|date=April 2011|title=The impact of DSM-IV symptom and clinical significance criteria on the prevalence estimates of subthreshold and threshold anxiety in the older adult population|journal=The American Journal of Geriatric Psychiatry|volume=19|issue=4|pages=316–26|doi=10.1097/JGP.0b013e3181ff416c|pmc=3682986|pmid=21427640}}</ref>

===ICD-11===
The diagnostic criteria for PMDD are also included in the [[ICD-11]], which has been in effect since 2022:<ref>{{Cite web |date= |title=ICD-11 – GA34.41 Premenstrual dysphoric disorder |url=https://icd.who.int/browse/latest-release/mms/en#1526774088 |access-date=2026-03-30 |website=icd.who.int |publisher=[[World Health Organization]]}}</ref>

* Affective, somatic, or cognitive symptoms occur regularly in conjunction with menstruation, beginning several days before the onset of menstruation.
* The symptoms include at least one affective symptom and at least one additional somatic or cognitive symptom.
* The symptoms are not a manifestation of another medical condition or mental disorder.
* The symptoms cause significant distress.

=== Other criteria sets ===
Other organizations that have published diagnostic criteria for PMDD include the [[Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists]] and the International Society for the Study of Premenstrual Disorders (ISPMD).<ref name=":82">{{cite journal | vauthors = O'Brien PM, Bäckström T, Brown C, Dennerstein L, Endicott J, Epperson CN, Eriksson E, Freeman E, Halbreich U, Ismail KM, Panay N, Pearlstein T, Rapkin A, Reid R, Schmidt P, Steiner M, Studd J, Yonkers K | display-authors = 6 | title = Towards a consensus on diagnostic criteria, measurement and trial design of the premenstrual disorders: the ISPMD Montreal consensus | journal = Archives of Women's Mental Health | volume = 14 | issue = 1 | pages = 13–21 | date = February 2011 | pmid = 21225438 | pmc = 4134928 | doi = 10.1007/s00737-010-0201-3 }}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.rcog.org.uk/en/guidelines-research-services/guidelines/gtg48/|title=Premenstrual Syndrome, Management (Green-top Guideline No. 48)|date=December 2016|website=Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists}}</ref> The ISPMD was a consensus group established by an international multidisciplinary group of experts. The group's diagnostic criteria for PMDD focus on the cyclic nature of the symptoms occurring during the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle, as well as the symptoms being absent after menstruation and before ovulation and causing significant impairment. The ISPMD diagnostic criteria for PMDD do not specify symptom characteristics or the number of symptoms.<ref name=":82" />

===Differential diagnosis===
Part of diagnosing PMDD is ruling out underlying psychiatric disorders or physical illnesses that can cause similar symptoms. That exhibits premenstrual exacerbation, the menopausal transition, hyperthyroidism, hypothyroidism, as well as other mood disorders. Furthermore, many medical disorders are worsened before menses, but these typically do not present strictly during the luteal phase.

'''Mood disorders''' – There is potential for patients to have psychiatric disorders with superimposed PMDD or psychiatric disorders. To establish the timeline of symptoms required for a diagnosis of PMDD symptoms need to be tracked using scales like the Calendar of Premenstrual Experiences or the Daily Record of Severity of Problems.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Feuerstein|first1=Michael|last2=Shaw|first2=William S.|date=April 2002|title=Measurement properties of the calendar of premenstrual experience in patients with premenstrual syndrome|journal=The Journal of Reproductive Medicine|volume=47|issue=4|pages=279–289|issn=0024-7758|pmid=12012879}}</ref>

'''Menopausal transition''' – affective symptoms associated with the menopausal transition most commonly start when the menstrual cycle starts to become irregular or anovulatory, whereas PMDD symptoms occur during the luteal phase of ovulatory cycles.

'''Thyroid disorders'''—Patients with both hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism may present with affective symptoms. The patient's history is very important to determine whether the provider should suspect thyroid disorders. Patients should also have thyroid hormone levels checked to ensure that no underlying thyroid disorder is present.

==Treatment==

=== Medication ===
Several medications have been shown to effectively reduce the physical and emotional symptoms of PMDD.

==== Antidepressant treatment ====
[[Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors]] (SSRIs) are the first-line medication.<ref name=":53" /><ref>{{Cite book|title=Encyclopedia of Clinical Neuropsychology| vauthors = Agyemang AA |publisher=Springer, Cham|year=2018|isbn=978-3-319-57111-9| veditors = Kreutzer JS, DeLuca J, Caplan B |location=Switzerland |doi=10.1007/978-3-319-57111-9}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal| vauthors = Eriksson E, Endicott J, Andersch B, Angst J, Demyttenaere K, Facchinetti F, Lanczik M, Montgomery S, Muscettola G, O'Brien PM, Studd J | display-authors = 6 |date=2002|title=New perspectives on the treatment of premenstrual syndrome and premenstrual dysphoric disorder|journal=Archives of Women's Mental Health|volume=4|issue=4|pages=111–119|doi=10.1007/s007370200009|s2cid=10427915|hdl=11380/16206|hdl-access=free}}</ref> Women taking SSRIs to ease PMDD generally report >50% alleviation in symptoms, which was significant improvement compared to placebo.<ref name=":4">{{cite journal|vauthors=Rapkin AJ, Winer SA|date=February 2008|title=The pharmacologic management of premenstrual dysphoric disorder|journal=Expert Opinion on Pharmacotherapy|volume=9|issue=3|pages=429–45|doi=10.1517/14656566.9.3.429|pmid=18220493|s2cid=72888643}}</ref> Two approaches to dosing have been studied: continuous dosing (daily) and luteal dosing (14 days before menstruation and discontinuing at the onset of menses).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Marjoribanks |first1=Jane |last2=Brown |first2=Julie |last3=O'Brien |first3=Patrick Michael Shaughn |last4=Wyatt |first4=Katrina |date=2013-06-07 |title=Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors for premenstrual syndrome |journal=The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews |volume=2013 |issue=6 |article-number=CD001396 |doi=10.1002/14651858.CD001396.pub3 |issn=1469-493X |pmc=7073417 |pmid=23744611}}</ref> Both dosing schedules have similar effectiveness, with some recent studies demonstrating greater symptom control with continuous dosing.<ref name="Rapkin" />

[[Serotonin–norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor|Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors]] (SNRIs) have also been studied in the treatment of PMDD and shown efficacy in reducing symptoms. These are alternatives for patients who do not respond to SSRIs. However, they are more likely to be dosed continuously due to SNRI discontinuation syndrome - a flu-like feeling caused by dropping blood levels of SNRI.<ref name="Rapkin" />

The rapid onset of anxiolytic and anti-dysphoric effects of SSRIs and SNRIs in PMDD contrast with their delayed efficacy in major depressive disorder (MDD).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Reilly |first1=Thomas J |last2=Wallman |first2=Phoebe |last3=Clark |first3=Ivana |last4=Knox |first4=Clare-Louise |last5=Craig |first5=Michael C |last6=Taylor |first6=David |date=March 2023 |title=Intermittent selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors for premenstrual syndromes: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised trials |journal=Journal of Psychopharmacology |volume=37 |issue=3 |pages=261–267 |doi=10.1177/02698811221099645 |issn=0269-8811 |pmid=35686687|pmc=10074750 }}</ref><ref name=":10">{{Cite journal |last1=Pearlstein |first1=Teri |last2=Steiner |first2=Meir |date=July 2008 |title=Premenstrual dysphoric disorder: burden of illness and treatment update |journal=Journal of Psychiatry & Neuroscience |volume=33 |issue=4 |pages=291–301 |doi=10.1139/jpn.0828 |issn=1180-4882 |pmc=2440788 |pmid=18592027}}</ref> Some SSRI and SNRIs have been demonstrated to induce inhibitory [[Neurosteroidogenesis|neurosteroid synthesis]], with a select few observed to do so at doses inactive on serotonin reuptake.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Griffin |first1=Lisa D. |last2=Mellon |first2=Synthia H. |date=1999-11-09 |title=Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors directly alter activity of neurosteroidogenic enzymes |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America |volume=96 |issue=23 |pages=13512–13517 |doi=10.1073/pnas.96.23.13512 |doi-access=free |issn=0027-8424 |pmid=10557352|pmc=23979 |bibcode=1999PNAS...9613512G }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Pinna |first1=Graziano |last2=Costa |first2=Erminio |last3=Guidotti |first3=Alessandro |date=June 2006 |title=Fluoxetine and norfluoxetine stereospecifically and selectively increase brain neurosteroid content at doses that are inactive on 5-HT reuptake |journal=Psychopharmacology |volume=186 |issue=3 |pages=362–372 |doi=10.1007/s00213-005-0213-2 |issn=0033-3158 |pmid=16432684}}</ref> It has been proposed that this effect may underlie the accelerated efficacy of SSRIs and SNRIs in PMDD relative to MDD.<ref name=":10"/>

==== Anxiolytics ====
Two medications typically given to reduce acute anxiety have been studied in treatment for PMDD: [[Benzodiazepine|alprazolam]] (Xanax) and [[buspirone]]. Alprazolam carries a risk of dependence and causes central nervous system depression, and results of clinical trials have not shown benefit to treatment.<ref name="Rapkin" /> Buspirone showed lower efficacy than SSRI, but may be used as an adjunctive treatment or alternative if SSRI side effects are intolerable to the patient.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Maharaj|first1=Shalini|last2=Trevino|first2=Kenneth|date=September 2015|title=A Comprehensive Review of Treatment Options for Premenstrual Syndrome and Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder|journal=Journal of Psychiatric Practice|volume=21|issue=5|pages=334–350|doi=10.1097/PRA.0000000000000099|issn=1538-1145|pmid=26352222|s2cid=12492648}}</ref>

=== Psychotherapy ===
[[Cognitive behavioral therapy]] (CBT) is effective for reducing premenstrual symptoms in women with (retrospectively-reported) PMS.<ref name="ReferenceA" /><ref name="Walker">{{cite journal |last1=Walker |first1=Kristy |title=Navigating PMDD through a trauma-informed lens. |journal=Therapy Today |date=May 2025 |volume=36 |issue=4 |page=44-46 |issn=1748-7846}}</ref> CBT is an evidence-based approach for treating depression and focuses on the link between mood, thoughts, and actions to help women address current issues and symptoms. When CBT was compared to SSRI alone or in combination with SSRI, groups receiving CBT had significant improvement of PMS symptoms.<ref name="ReferenceA" /> Through the practice of CBT, women are better able to recognize and modify recurrent issues as well as thought and behavior patterns that interfere with functioning well or that make depressive symptoms worse. However, a recent meta-analysis suggests that existing psychotherapies may be primarily useful for reducing impairment (rather than symptom severity) in PMDD.<ref name="ReferenceA" />

=== Hormonal treatment ===
[[Oral contraceptives]] have been effective in reducing PMS symptoms, but only certain formulations have proven to be modestly effective in the treatment of PMDD.<ref name=":4" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Freeman|first1=Ellen W.|last2=Halbreich|first2=Uriel|last3=Grubb|first3=Gary S.|last4=Rapkin|first4=Andrea J.|last5=Skouby|first5=Sven O.|last6=Smith|first6=Lynne|last7=Mirkin|first7=Sebastian|last8=Constantine|first8=Ginger D.|date=May 2012|title=An overview of four studies of a continuous oral contraceptive (levonorgestrel 90 mcg/ethinyl estradiol 20 mcg) on premenstrual dysphoric disorder and premenstrual syndrome|journal=Contraception|volume=85|issue=5|pages=437–445|doi=10.1016/j.contraception.2011.09.010|issn=1879-0518|pmid=22152588}}</ref> Transdermal estrogen and intrauterine devices containing levonorgestrel have also had modest efficacy.<ref name="Rapkin" />

Another FDA-approved treatment for women with premenstrual dysphoric disorder who have impairments with function is an oral contraceptive with [[ethinylestradiol]] and [[drospirenone]] (progestin) taken on a 24-4 schedule (24 active pills, 4 inactive pills).<ref name=":3">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XozHCQAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA32|title=Maternal-Child Nursing Care|vauthors=Ward S|publisher=F.A. Davis Company|year=2016|isbn=978-0-8036-3665-1|location=Philadelphia, PA, USA|page=32}}</ref> Hormonal birth control containing drospirenone and low levels of estrogen (ethinylestradiol) helps relieve severe symptoms related to premenstrual dysphoric disorder, for at least the first three months that it is used. It is not clear if this approach is effective for more than three menstrual cycles.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ma |first1=Siyan |last2=Song |first2=Sae Jin |date=2023-06-23 |title=Oral contraceptives containing drospirenone for premenstrual syndrome |url= |journal=The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews |volume=2023 |issue=6 |article-number=CD006586 |doi=10.1002/14651858.CD006586.pub5 |issn=1469-493X |pmc=10289136 |pmid=37365881 }}</ref> The placebo effect has not been ruled out. The idea behind using oral contraceptives is to suppress ovulation, therefore suppressing sex hormone fluctuations.

Another treatment, typically used when other options have failed, is an injection of a gonadotropin-releasing hormone(GnRH) agonist with adjunctive estrogen and progesterone or tibolone. This is among last resorts because GnRH antagonists can cause medical menopause by shutting down the body's pathway for reproductive hormones called the hypothalamic, pituitary, gonadal axis. As a result, GnRH therapy presents an increased risk of osteopenia (decreased bone density) and cardiovascular disease. This therapy is often reserved for patients considering surgical menopause to test the outcome of the surgery.<ref name="Rapkin" />

=== Surgical menopause ===
In a minority of patient who meet specific criteria and drug-based treatments are ineffective or produce significant side effects, hysterectomy and bilateral oophorectomy followed by estrogen replacement therapy is an option<ref name=":0">{{cite journal | vauthors = Reid RL | title = When should surgical treatment be considered for premenstrual dysphoric disorder? | journal = Menopause International | volume = 18 | issue = 2 | pages = 77–81 | date = June 2012 | pmid = 22611227 | doi = 10.1258/mi.2012.012009 | s2cid = 21181483 }}</ref> Typically, the [[Hysterectomy|uterus is removed]] during the same surgery, and the woman is prescribed a low-dose [[estrogen patch]] to reduce the symptoms produced by [[Surgical menopause|surgically induced menopause]].<ref name=":0" /> There are five guidelines that should be considered before undergoing surgical treatment.<ref name="pmid15458909">{{cite journal | vauthors = Johnson SR | title = Premenstrual syndrome, premenstrual dysphoric disorder, and beyond: a clinical primer for practitioners | journal = Obstet Gynecol | volume = 104 | issue = 4 | pages = 845–59 | date = October 2004 | pmid = 15458909 | doi = 10.1097/01.AOG.0000140686.66212.1e }}</ref>

* The diagnosis of PMDD must be confirmed
* [[Gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist|GnRH agonist]] therapy must be the only medical therapy that has been effective, and it must have been effective continuously for a minimum of six months
* Tolerance of estrogen replacement therapy has been tested
* The woman does not desire children
* The woman's age warrants several more years of therapy

=== Adjunctive and alternative treatments ===
Other proposed treatments include dietary modification, herbal remedies including St John's Wort and chasteberry, acupuncture, and exercise.<ref name="Rapkin" /> Some evidence suggests caffeine, sugar and alcohol intake may increase PMS symptoms.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Cunningham|first1=Joanne|last2=Yonkers|first2=Kimberly Ann|last3=O'Brien|first3=Shaughn|last4=Eriksson|first4=Elias|date=2009|title=Update on research and treatment of premenstrual dysphoric disorder|journal=Harvard Review of Psychiatry|volume=17|issue=2|pages=120–137|doi=10.1080/10673220902891836|issn=1465-7309|pmc=3098121|pmid=19373620}}</ref> A review article claimed significant improvement of PMS symptoms with herbal treatments and acupuncture but the studies selected for review did not stratify severity of symptoms.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Jang|first1=Su Hee|last2=Kim|first2=Dong Il|last3=Choi|first3=Min-Sun|date=2014-01-10|title=Effects and treatment methods of acupuncture and herbal medicine for premenstrual syndrome/premenstrual dysphoric disorder: systematic review|journal=BMC Complementary and Alternative Medicine|volume=14|article-number=11|doi=10.1186/1472-6882-14-11|issn=1472-6882|pmc=3898234|pmid=24410911 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Finally, the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists recommends regular aerobic exercise to reduce PMS symptoms.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Daley|first=Amanda|date=June 2009|title=Exercise and premenstrual symptomatology: a comprehensive review|journal=Journal of Women's Health |volume=18|issue=6|pages=895–899|doi=10.1089/jwh.2008.1098|issn=1931-843X|pmid=19514832}}</ref> The first trials have been approved to study microdosing [[LSD]] for the treatment of PMS and PMDD.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Carreon |first=Mary |date=2024-06-18 |title=LSD Is on Deck As New Treatment for PMS and PMDD |url=https://doubleblindmag.com/lsd-for-pms-pmdd/ |access-date=2025-02-28 |website=DoubleBlind Mag |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Taylor |first=Eleanor |date=2024-03-18 |title=Psilocybin shows promise in supporting symptoms of Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder |url=https://www.drugscience.org.uk/psilocybin-shows-promise-in-supporting-symptoms-of-premenstrual-dysphoric-disorder |access-date=2025-02-28 |website=Drug Science |language=en}}</ref>

==History==
In the 18th century, there were early accounts of weeping and other symptoms recurring almost every month,<ref name="pmid10954967">{{cite journal | vauthors = Stolberg M | title = The monthly malady: a history of premenstrual suffering | journal = Medical History | volume = 44 | issue = 3 | pages = 301–22 | date = July 2000 | pmid = 10954967 | pmc = 1044286 | doi = 10.1017/S0025727300066722| url = }}</ref> and in 1822 Prichard gave this description: "Many women ... display a degree of excitement and irritation ... at the period of menstruation; these are chiefly females of very irritable habits. In such instances ... an unusual vehemence of feeling and expression is observed ... or there is torpor and dejection of mind with a despondent disposition".<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Prichard JC | date = 1822 | title = A Treatise on Diseases of the Nervous System. | location = London | publisher = Underwood | volume = 1| page = 195 }}</ref> In 1827, a German mother was acquitted of infanticide on the grounds of a menstrual mood disorder.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hitzig JE | date = 1827 | title = Mord in einem durch Eintreten des Monatsflusses herbeigeführten unfreien Zustande. | journal = Zeitschrift für Criminal-rechts-pflege in den Preussischen Staaten | volume = 12 | pages = 239–331 }}</ref> Premenstrual tension was also described in the French literature of the early 19th century.<ref>{{cite book | vauthors = Brière de Boisment A | date = 1842 | title = De la Menstruation considerée dans ses Rapports Physiologiques et Pathologiques. | location = Paris | publisher = Baillière | page = 37 }}</ref> Nearly one hundred years later, there were American descriptions of a cyclic personality change appearing 10–14 days before, and ending dramatically at the menses.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Israel SL | title = Premenstrual tension. | journal = Journal of the American Medical Association | date = May 1938 | volume = 110 | issue = 21 | pages = 1721–3 | doi = 10.1001/jama.1938.02790210001001 }}</ref>

The diagnostic category was discussed in the [[Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders#DSM-III-R .281987.29|DSM-IIIR]] (1987), in which the proposed condition was named "Late Luteal Phase Dysphoric Disorder" and was included in the appendix as a proposed diagnostic category needing further study.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 3rd Edition Revised |publisher=American Psychiatric Association |year=1987 |doi=10.1176/appi.books.9780890420188.dsm-iii-r |isbn=0-89042-018-1 }}</ref> Preparations for the [[Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders#DSM-IV .281994.29|DSM-IV]] led to debate about whether to keep the category at all, only keep it in the appendix, or remove it entirely; the reviewers determined that the condition was still too poorly studied and defined, so it was kept in the appendix but elaborated with diagnostic criteria to aid further study.<ref name=":16" /><ref name="VV2000">{{cite news |url=http://www.villagevoice.com/news/sarafem-nation-6416934 |title=Sarafem Nation | vauthors = Spartos C |date=December 5, 2000 |work=Village Voice |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180617043055/https://www.villagevoice.com/2000/12/05/sarafem-nation/ |archive-date=2018-06-17 |url-status=dead |name-list-style=vanc}}</ref>

As preparations were underway in 1998 for the [[Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders#DSM-IV-TR .282000.29|DSM-IV-TR]], the conversation changed, as [[Eli Lilly and Company]] paid for a large clinical trial of [[fluoxetine]] as a potential treatment for the condition that was then conducted by Canadian academics and published in the [[New England Journal of Medicine]] in 1995.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 4th Edition |publisher=American Psychiatric Association |year=2000 |doi=10.1176/appi.books.9780890420249.dsm-iv-tr |isbn=0-89042-024-6 }}</ref> Other studies have been conducted as well, wherein all found that approximately 60% of women with PMDD in the trials improved with the drug; representatives from Lilly & Co. and the FDA participated in the discussion.<ref name=":16" /><ref name="VV2000" />

Various strong stances were taken in the said discussion. Sally Severino, a psychiatrist, argued that because symptoms were more prevalent in the United States, PMDD was a [[culture-bound syndrome]] and not a biological condition; she also claimed it unnecessarily pathologized the hormonal changes of the menstrual cycle.<ref name=":16" /> Jean Endicott, another psychiatrist and chair of the committee, has argued that it was a valid condition from which women suffer and should be diagnosed and treated, and has claimed that if the symptoms were felt by males, far more effort and research would have been done by that moment. In the end, the committee kept PMDD in the appendix.<ref name=":16" />

The decision has been criticized as being driven by Lilly's financial interests, and possibly by financial interests of members of the committee who had received funding from Lilly.<ref name=":16" /> [[Paula Caplan]], a psychologist who had served on the committee for the DSM-IV, noted at the time of the DSM-IV-TR decision that there was evidence that calcium supplements could treat PMDD but the committee gave it no attention. She had also claimed that the diagnostic category is harmful to women with PMDD, leading them to believe they are mentally ill and potentially leading others to mistrust them in situations as important as job promotions or child custody cases.<ref name=":16" /> She has called PMDD a fake disorder.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Steiner M, Steinberg S, Stewart D, Carter D, Berger C, Reid R, Grover D, Streiner D | display-authors = 6 | title = Fluoxetine in the treatment of premenstrual dysphoria. Canadian Fluoxetine/Premenstrual Dysphoria Collaborative Study Group | journal = The New England Journal of Medicine | volume = 332 | issue = 23 | pages = 1529–34 | date = June 1995 | pmid = 7739706 | doi = 10.1056/NEJM199506083322301 | doi-access = free }}</ref> [[Nada Stotland]] has expressed concern that women with PMDD may have another serious condition like [[major depressive disorder]] or may be facing difficult circumstances—such domestic abuse—and therefore may have their true issues remain undiagnosed and mismanaged if their gynecologist diagnoses them with PMDD and gives them drugs to treat it.<ref name=":16" />

The validity of PMDD was once more heavily debated when it came time to create the [[DSM-5]] in 2008.<ref>{{Cite journal | vauthors = Caplan PJ |date=2004 |title=The Debate About PMDD and Sarafem |journal=Women & Therapy |volume=27 |issue=3–4 |pages=55–67 |doi=10.1300/J015v27n03_05|s2cid=141754567}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/ref/health/healthguide/esn-pms-ess.html |title=A Clash of Science and Politics Over PMS | vauthors = Chen I |date=18 December 2008 |work=The New York Times |access-date=27 April 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110123084817/https://www.nytimes.com/ref/health/healthguide/esn-pms-ess.html |archive-date=2011-01-23}}</ref> In the end it was moved out of the appendix and into the main text as a formal category. A review in the [[Journal of Clinical Psychiatry]] published in 2014 examined the arguments against inclusion, which it summarized as:

# the PMDD label will harm women economically, politically, legally, and domestically;
# there is no equivalent hormone-based medical label for males;
# the research on PMDD is faulty;
# PMDD is a culture-bound condition;
# PMDD is due to situational, rather than biological, factors; and
# PMDD was fabricated by pharmaceutical companies for financial gain.<ref name=":17">{{cite journal | vauthors = Hartlage SA, Breaux CA, Yonkers KA | title = Addressing concerns about the inclusion of premenstrual dysphoric disorder in DSM-5 | journal = The Journal of Clinical Psychiatry | volume = 75 | issue = 1 | pages = 70–6 | date = January 2014 | pmid = 24345853 | doi = 10.4088/JCP.13cs08368 }}</ref> 
Each argument was addressed, and researchers found:
# No evidence of harm;
# no equivalent hormone-driven disorder has been discovered in men despite research seeking it;
# the research base has matured, and many more reputable studies have been performed;
# several cases of PMDD have been reported or identified;
# a small minority of women do have the condition; and
# While there has been a financial conflict of interest, it has not made the available research unusable.<ref name=yonkers /><ref name=":17" />

It concluded that women have historically been under-treated and told that they were making their symptoms up, and that the formal diagnostic criteria would spur more funding, research, diagnosis, and treatment for women with PMDD.<ref name=":17" />

==See also==
* [[List of investigational PMS/PMDD drugs]]
